text.title: OpenAI API Integration — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the openai-api methodology. Sources: OpenAI API reference (platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference); OpenAI Structured Outputs guide; OpenAI Batch API documentation.

rule.statement: Pin exact model versions (`gpt-4.1-2025-04-14`, not `gpt-4-turbo` or `gpt-4o`); rotate explicitly in PRs after eval review.
rule.rationale: Aliases silently roll under you; one Sunday the eval suite regresses 5% and there is no signal until a customer complains.

rule.statement: For new projects, use the Responses API with `previous_response_id` for multi-turn flows; reserve Chat Completions for legacy code that already shipped.
rule.rationale: Responses API exposes prompt caching, server-side context, and the new tool-call shape — switching costs grow with code base age.

rule.statement: Use `client.beta.chat.completions.parse(response_format=Model)` (Structured Outputs) instead of JSON mode; the schema is enforced server-side.
rule.rationale: JSON mode just promises valid JSON, not your schema; Structured Outputs guarantee the model output matches the Pydantic schema.

rule.statement: Set `max_tokens` (or `max_output_tokens`) on every call; never rely on the model default.

rationale.source: OpenAI prompt caching / Responses API docs

rule.rationale: Default ceilings change between model versions and cost spikes hide behind verbose models that suddenly emit 4k tokens for a yes/no answer.

rule.statement: Route all non-interactive workloads through the Batch API (50% discount); reserve real-time endpoints for user-facing latency-critical paths.
rule.rationale: Batch API is a no-brainer cost reduction for any workload where 24h latency is acceptable; production systems leave money on the table by skipping it.
